react-stable-timeline.title: @mogiyoon/react-stable-timeline
react-stable-timeline.subtitle: A React timeline library where event card rows stay locked through pan and zoom
react-stable-timeline.description:
A React timeline component library that lays events side by side along a time axis, the way a chronological catalog page would. It started on vis-timeline, but the same event card kept hopping to a different row whenever the user panned or zoomed, so the row-placement pseudocode was authored in Korean by hand, reimplemented from scratch, and then extracted as a standalone library package.

Distributed as a React 18+ / TypeScript package bundled with tsup. Label widths are measured directly in the browser, eliminating the label collisions that character-count estimates produced in Hangul- and CJK-mixed text. A viewport-clamp bug surfaced while validating the library inside Storytect (the worldbuilding editor that consumes it) was patched in 0.2.1.

overview.introduction: A React timeline component library where event card rows stay locked through pan and zoom
overview.features: Full-dataset first-fit row placement, Canvas2D label width measurement, zoom controls, trackpad-friendly interaction, controlled and uncontrolled viewport modes

0.subtitle: Switching to a Custom Implementation Because Rows Kept Reshuffling on Pan and Zoom
0.0: The timeline component for the chronological catalog page was first built on vis-timeline. The stacking behavior that prevents events at the same time from colliding worked correctly, but since row positions were recalculated against only the currently visible viewport, event cards shifted rows during pan and zoom.
0.1: Keeping each row assignment stable through pan and zoom was judged to be the better UX. Since the mismatch was at the level of the placement rule itself rather than a tunable option, vis-timeline was removed in favor of a custom implementation that includes a row-stable mode.

1.subtitle: Designing the Row-Stable Placement Algorithm and Its TypeScript Implementation
1.0: The core of the row-stable mode is separating row computation from viewport movement: rows are decided once against the full dataset, and pan/zoom only translate coordinates afterwards. The hand-written pseudocode assumes a queue A (an ordered list of items to process) and a dictionary B (a key-value map of each row index to its rightmost end point). It reads: "Sort items into queue A by (start time, name). Initialize dictionary B keyed by row index, valued by end point. Pop component C, compare its start to B[0]'s end point — if C starts later, place it on row 0 and update the end point; otherwise iterate the dictionary. If no row fits, insert into the row whose end-point distance is smallest in absolute value."
1.1: The actual implementation was refined to map queue A onto a (start, label)-sorted array and dictionary B onto a rowEndsPx array. The original proposal said "insert into the closest row," but the implementation opens a new row instead to avoid label collisions. Time complexity is O(n log n) for the sort plus O(n·R) for placement, and the result is deterministic — the same input always produces the same output.

2.subtitle: Measuring Real Pixel Widths Instead of Estimating from Character Count
2.0: Character-count-based estimates underrepresented Hangul's actual pixel width by about 30%, leaving visible collisions between labels. To close that gap, Canvas2D measureText (the browser's text-width API) is used, and a measurer function wraps it so the value can flow into the placement calculation.
2.1: When a label is wider than its time slot, the label width drives the gap calculation against the next event card to prevent overlap; when the time slot is wider, the slot width is used instead.

3.subtitle: Cursor-Anchored Zoom and Multiple Pan Inputs
3.0: Zoom ranges from 100% (fit, the default ratio that puts the entire dataset on screen) up to 5000%. The zoomFactor value tunes the per-step multiplier anywhere between 1.05 and 1.5.
3.1: Panning supports two-finger horizontal scroll and Shift + vertical wheel. Setting zoomStable to true freezes the row layout at fit zoom so rows stay still through every zoom level afterwards; the default is false, in which case rows recompute against the current viewport on each zoom.

0.subtitle: Hangul Label Widths Underestimated by ~30% via Character-Count Estimation
0.0: Problem: With character-count-based label width estimation, Hangul's actual pixel width was calculated about 30% smaller than reality, leaving visible collisions between adjacent labels.
0.1:
Resolution

	1. Compared the estimate against the real measurement across various font and text combinations.
	2. Adopted Canvas2D measureText to obtain pixel widths that reflect the current font settings.
0.2: Result: Label widths now measure accurately across Hangul, Latin, and mixed-script text, and the label collisions are gone.

0.1: Published a first-fit row placement library on NPM, building on hand-designed data structures and algorithm and optimizing the implementation together with AI
0.2: Eliminated label collisions in Hangul, CJK, and mixed-script text via Canvas2D measurement, and exported the first-fit placement function so layouts can also be precomputed in a worker

1.1: Recognized that the mismatch was at the assumption level — not a tunable option — and built a tool that fits the use case instead of bending the use case to fit the library
1.2: Saw clearly that the queue + dictionary pseudocode was already a definition of first-fit interval partitioning, while the actual implementation was a joint effort with AI
1.3: Experienced firsthand a cross-project feedback loop where the consumer (Storytect) surfaced the controlled-mode clamp bug almost immediately after validation began

features.name: 📌 Stable Row Placement
features.description: First-fit placement over the full dataset keeps every event card on the same row through pan and zoom

features.name: 🔤 Precise Label Width
features.description: Canvas2D measureText measures Hangul, CJK, and mixed-script text accurately

features.name: 🔍 Zoom Controls
features.description: Supports 100% (fit) to 5000% zoom with cursor-anchored zoom

features.name: 🖱 Trackpad Interaction
features.description: Two-finger horizontal scroll panning, Cmd/Ctrl + wheel anchored zoom, and drag panning all supported

features.name: 🎚 Controlled / Uncontrolled
features.description: Two-way binding with an external timebar via viewportStart/End props, or self-managed mode, both supported

developmentProcess.title: Problem Identification
developmentProcess.content: Recognized that vis-timeline's viewport-relative row recomputation caused event cards to jump rows under pan and zoom

developmentProcess.title: Algorithm Design + Implementation
developmentProcess.content: The user authored the queue + dictionary first-fit pseudocode in Korean by hand; Claude carried out the TypeScript implementation and the Canvas2D label measurement infrastructure

developmentProcess.title: Library Extraction + Validation
developmentProcess.content: Extracted Storytect's internal timeline component as a standalone npm package bundled with tsup and shipped with TypeScript type definitions, then iterated up to 0.2.1 through the consumer-side validation cycle

claudeInfo.details: While working on Storytect, the user designed the queue + dictionary first-fit pseudocode in Korean by hand. Claude Code CLI as a single agent carried out the TypeScript implementation, the Canvas2D label-measurement infrastructure, the hooks-and-subcomponents refactor, and the documentation. Only the overflow case diverged from the user's proposal — instead of "insert into the closest row," the implementation opens a new row to avoid label collisions. The controlled-mode clamp bug surfaced during in-app validation was fixed using a one-line patch the user wrote directly in the prompt.
